Now, let's look at the second activator of liking, Praise. How simple praise works to influence people is illustrated wonderfully by the actions of Guinness Book World Record holder Joe Gerrard. Joe was the number one car salesman in the United States for twelve years straight, because he sold an average of more than five cars or trucks every day that he worked. That's an incredible number. Joe exemplifies the fact that it is not only true that we like those who are like us, but we also like those who do like us. And say so. Each month, Gerrard sent each of his more than thirteen thousand former customers a holiday greeting card appropriate for that month, such as Happy Thanksgiving, or Happy Independence Day, or Happy New Year's Day. But inside the card there was a printed message that never varied. It always said, I like you, and contain nothing else besides this form of praise except Joe's name. I like you. It came in the mail every year, twelve times a year like clockwork. I like you. On a printed card that went to thirteen thousand other people too. What a statement of liking so impersonal, so obviously designed to sell cars work. Joe Gerrard thought so, and a man as successful as he was at what he did deserves our attention. Joe understood an important fact about human nature. As a rule, people believe praise and like those who give it to them. Because praise can be either sincere or insincere, we find ourselves with a smuggler versus sleuth choice to make. On the smuggler side, although manufacturing a false compliment may make the recipient like you more, it will make you lose an even more powerful tool of influence. The chance to like the person you are complimenting. Look at it this way. If as a sleuth you take the time and effort to find just one truly praiseworthy thing to say about the person you want to influence, you will be like more for it. But in addition, you'll gain the added benefit of liking that individual more. And once the people you're dealing with recognize that you like them, something special happens. Down go the defenses. Down go their cautions about whether they can trust what you say. Why? Because everybody knows that we don't take advantage of the people we like. The consequence is that when you interact with them in the future, you'll do so while liking them more, which is something they're going to be able to sense. And that's going to make them trust that you will do your best for them. And they'll be right. Because as much as we might not want to admit it, we all, each of us, do our best for the people we like.
